text.title: Multi-Burn-Rate SLO Recipe Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of multi-burn-rate-slo-recipe enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Every burn-rate alert pairs a short window (5m / 1h) with a long window (30m / 6h) — both must breach to page.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook — Alerting on SLOs

rule.rationale: Short alone yields noise; long alone yields slow response. The pairing balances both.

rule.statement: Burn-rate thresholds are derived from the SLO target: fast burn ≈ 14.4×, slow burn ≈ 6× (for 30-day window).
rule.rationale: Fixed numeric thresholds without SLO derivation drift as SLOs change.

rule.statement: Every alert links to a runbook with diagnostic + remediation steps; the runbook is reviewed quarterly.
rule.rationale: Alert without runbook = paged engineer guessing.

rule.statement: Alerts route to a single owning on-call rotation; multi-team routing requires escalation rules, not parallel paging.
rule.rationale: Multi-team paging causes 'somebody else has it' diffusion.

rule.statement: Alert volume is tuned: a one-week dry run with the alerts in 'observe-only' mode precedes go-live; thresholds adjusted if >5 false positives per week.
rule.rationale: Going live without tuning is how teams disable alerts permanently.

rule.statement: If preconditions in `Applies If` are not all true, skip this methodology and route to the adjacent one referenced in Related.
rule.rationale: A skip rule keeps the decision tree closed: every branch ends at a real rule id.
